title: Scholarship Matching Agent
problem: Students miss out on scholarships because the matching process requires researching hundreds of programs and comparing eligibility criteria manually.
solution: Agent ingests student profile and academic background, searches a scholarship knowledge base, scores matches on eligibility and fit, and produces a ranked list with application guidance for each.
whoItsFor: Students, Academic Counselors, Financial Aid Offices
workflowSteps: Student submits profile: GPA, major, demographics, interests, financial need
Agent searches scholarship knowledge base across multiple categories
Scores each match on: eligibility (hard filter), fit (semantic), deadline proximity
Produces ranked shortlist with eligibility summary, application steps, and deadline for each
Optionally: drafts first-pass personal statement outline for top matches
keyInsights: Hybrid search (hard eligibility filters + semantic fit scoring) is the key pattern. Pure semantic search returns false positives on eligibility-gated scholarships. The pre-filter-then-rank pattern from RAG/RBAC applies directly here.

relatedExams.why: Applications and Integration — hybrid search, pre-filter patterns
Prompting and Task Execution — structured output, ranking

mermaidDiagram:
flowchart TD
  A([Student Profile Submitted]) --> B{Hard Eligibility Filter}
  B -->|Eligible| C[Semantic Fit Scoring]
  B -->|Ineligible| X([Excluded])
  C --> D[Score by Fit + Deadline Proximity]
  D --> E[Ranked Shortlist]
  E --> F([Application Guidance per Match])
architectureNotes: Applicant profiles (demographics, financials, academic records, essays) are embedded and matched against a scholarship catalog using vector similarity. A scoring model ranks scholarships by eligibility fit. For top matches, the agent drafts personalised application guidance and identifies missing documents. A counsellor review step approves the shortlist before it is shared with the student.
